title: Design a URL Shortener
hint: Think about hashing, base62 encoding, and read-heavy traffic patterns.
answer: Use a hash/counter to generate short codes, store mappings in a key-value store (e.g., Redis + DB), use 301 redirects, add caching for hot URLs, and partition by short-code prefix for scale.

title: Design a Rate Limiter
hint: Consider token bucket vs sliding window algorithms.
answer: Use a token bucket or sliding window counter per user/IP. Store counts in Redis with TTL. Return 429 when exceeded. Place at API gateway level. Use distributed counters for multi-node setups.

title: Design a Chat Application
hint: Think about WebSockets, message ordering, and presence.
answer: Use WebSockets for real-time delivery. Store messages in a partitioned DB (by chat ID). Use a message queue (Kafka) for fan-out. Track presence via heartbeats. Use last-write-wins or vector clocks for ordering.

title: Design a News Feed System
hint: Consider push vs pull models and ranking algorithms.
answer: Fan-out on write for users with few followers (push to followers' feeds). Fan-out on read for celebrities. Store feeds in Redis sorted sets. Rank by relevance + recency. Pre-compute feeds asynchronously.

title: Design a Distributed Cache
hint: Think about consistent hashing, eviction policies, and replication.
answer: Use consistent hashing to distribute keys across nodes. Apply LRU/LFU eviction. Replicate to N nodes for availability. Use gossip protocol for membership. Handle cache stampede with locking or probabilistic early expiry.

title: Design a File Storage Service
hint: Think about chunking, deduplication, and metadata storage.
answer: Split files into chunks (4-8MB). Deduplicate via content hashing (SHA-256). Store chunks in blob storage, metadata in a relational DB. Use CDN for downloads. Support resumable uploads via chunk tracking.

title: Design a Notification System
hint: Consider multiple channels, priorities, and user preferences.
answer: Use a message queue to decouple senders from delivery. Route by channel (push, email, SMS, in-app). Store user preferences. Apply rate limiting per user. Use priority queues for urgent notifications. Track delivery status.

title: Design a Search Autocomplete
hint: Think about tries, ranking by frequency, and latency requirements.
answer: Build a trie from popular queries. Rank suggestions by frequency, recency, and personalization. Cache top results per prefix in Redis. Update asynchronously from search logs. Keep p99 latency under 100ms.

title: Design a Ride-Sharing Service
hint: Think about geospatial indexing, matching, and real-time tracking.
answer: Use geohashing or quadtrees for spatial indexing of drivers. Match riders to nearest available drivers. Use WebSockets for real-time location updates. Store trip data in a relational DB. Use ETA prediction with ML models.

title: Design a Video Streaming Platform
hint: Consider transcoding, adaptive bitrate, and CDN distribution.
answer: Transcode uploads into multiple resolutions/codecs asynchronously. Use adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH). Distribute via CDN edge nodes. Store metadata in DB, videos in blob storage. Use chunked upload for large files.

title: Design a Metrics Collection System
hint: Think about time-series data, aggregation, and high write throughput.
answer: Ingest via UDP/HTTP to a write-optimized time-series DB (like InfluxDB). Pre-aggregate at collection agents. Use retention policies to downsample old data. Support flexible queries with tags/labels. Buffer writes in a message queue.

title: Design an API Gateway
hint: Think about routing, authentication, rate limiting, and observability.
answer: Route requests by path/header to backend services. Handle auth (JWT validation), rate limiting, request transformation, and circuit breaking. Log all requests for observability. Cache responses where possible. Deploy as a reverse proxy cluster.

title: Design a Distributed Task Queue
hint: Consider at-least-once delivery, retries, and dead letter queues.
answer: Use a message broker (RabbitMQ/SQS). Workers pull tasks and ACK on completion. Retry with exponential backoff on failure. Move poison messages to a dead letter queue. Partition by task type for isolation. Track task state in a DB.

title: Design a Key-Value Store
hint: Think about partitioning, replication, and consistency trade-offs.
answer: Partition keys via consistent hashing. Replicate to N nodes. Use quorum reads/writes (R+W>N) for consistency. Resolve conflicts with vector clocks or last-write-wins. Use SSTables + memtables (LSM tree) for storage. Compact in background.

title: Design a Payment System
hint: Think about idempotency, double-spend prevention, and reconciliation.
answer: Use idempotency keys on every request. Process payments in a state machine (pending → authorized → captured → settled). Use distributed locks to prevent double-spend. Log every state transition. Reconcile with payment providers daily. Use event sourcing for auditability.

title: Design a Content Delivery Network
hint: Think about edge caching, cache invalidation, and origin shielding.
answer: Deploy edge servers in multiple PoPs worldwide. Cache content by URL + headers. Use TTL-based expiry with purge API for invalidation. Shield origin with a mid-tier cache. Route users to nearest PoP via DNS/anycast. Support push and pull caching models.

title: Design a Recommendation Engine
hint: Consider collaborative filtering, content-based, and hybrid approaches.
answer: Use collaborative filtering (users who liked X also liked Y) for warm users. Content-based filtering for cold start. Compute offline in batch (Spark) and serve from a feature store. Re-rank in real-time with user context. A/B test different models.

title: Design a Distributed Lock Service
hint: Think about fencing tokens, TTLs, and consensus algorithms.
answer: Use a consensus-based system (etcd/ZooKeeper) to elect lock holders. Attach fencing tokens to prevent stale locks from acting. Use TTLs to auto-release on crash. Support read-write locks for higher concurrency. Monitor lock contention metrics.

title: Design a Web Crawler
hint: Think about politeness, URL frontier, and deduplication.
answer: Use a URL frontier (priority queue) with politeness delays per domain. Deduplicate URLs with a Bloom filter. Parse HTML for new links. Store content in blob storage, metadata in DB. Distribute work across crawler nodes via consistent hashing on domain.

title: Design an Email Service
hint: Think about queuing, deliverability, bounce handling, and templates.
answer: Queue outgoing emails via a message broker. Send through multiple SMTP relays for throughput. Handle bounces and complaints via webhooks. Use SPF/DKIM/DMARC for deliverability. Support templates with variable substitution. Track open/click rates via pixel/redirect.
